# Constitution of the Cayman Islands - Full Text

> Source: constitution.ky
> This is the complete text of the Cayman Islands Constitution Order 2009 for LLM consumption.
> For official legal reference: https://www.legislation.gov.uk/uksi/2009/1379/schedule/2/made

---

## Part I - Bill of Rights, Freedoms and Responsibilities

### Section 1 - Guarantee of Rights, Freedoms and Responsibilities

This Bill of Rights, Freedoms and Responsibilities is a cornerstone of democracy in the Cayman Islands.

It enshrines the rights and freedoms to which every person in the Cayman Islands is entitled, and imposes responsibilities upon those persons.

### Section 2 - Life

Everyone's right to life shall be protected by law.

No one shall be deprived of his or her life intentionally save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he or she has been convicted.

Deprivation of life shall not be regarded as inflicted in contravention of this section when it results from the use of force which is no more than absolutely necessary in defence of any person from unlawful violence, in order to effect a lawful arrest, or in action lawfully taken for the purpose of suppressing a riot, insurrection or mutiny.

### Section 3 - Torture and Inhuman Treatment

No person shall be subjected to torture or inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment.

### Section 4 - Slavery or Forced Labour

No person shall be held in slavery or servitude.

No person shall be required to perform forced or compulsory labour.

For the purposes of this section, forced or compulsory labour does not include any labour required in consequence of the sentence or order of a court, any service of a military character, any service required in the event of an emergency or calamity threatening the life or well-being of the community, or any labour or service which forms part of normal civic obligations.

### Section 5 - Personal Liberty

No person shall be deprived of his or her personal liberty save as may be authorised by law in specified circumstances.

Any person who is arrested or detained shall be informed promptly of the reasons for his or her arrest or detention and shall be entitled to communicate with a legal practitioner of his or her choice.

Any person who is arrested or detained and is not tried within a reasonable time shall be released either unconditionally or upon reasonable conditions to ensure that the person appears for trial at a later date.

### Section 6 - Treatment of Prisoners

All persons deprived of their liberty have the right to be treated with humanity and with respect for the inherent dignity of the human person.

### Section 7 - Fair Trial

In the determination of his or her civil rights and obligations or of any criminal charge against him or her, every person is entitled to a fair and public hearing within a reasonable time by an independent and impartial court or tribunal established by law.

Every person charged with a criminal offence shall be presumed innocent until proved guilty according to law.

Every person charged with a criminal offence has the following minimum rights: to be informed promptly of the nature and cause of the accusation against him or her; to have adequate time and facilities for the preparation of his or her defence; to defend himself or herself in person or through legal assistance; to examine or have examined witnesses against him or her; and to have the free assistance of an interpreter if he or she cannot understand or speak the language used in court.

### Section 8 - No Punishment Without Law

No one shall be held guilty of any criminal offence on account of any act or omission which did not constitute a criminal offence under any law at the time when it was committed.

No penalty shall be imposed for any criminal offence that is heavier than the maximum penalty that was applicable at the time the criminal offence was committed.

### Section 9 - Private and Family Life

Government shall respect every person's right to private and family life, his or her home and his or her correspondence.

There shall be no interference by government with the exercise of this right except such as is in accordance with the law and is necessary in a democratic society in the interests of national security, public safety or the economic well-being of the country, for the prevention of disorder or crime, for the protection of health or morals, or for the protection of the rights and freedoms of others.

### Section 10 - Conscience and Religion

No person shall be hindered by government in the enjoyment of his or her freedom of conscience.

Freedom of conscience includes freedom of thought and of religion, freedom to change his or her religion or belief and freedom, either alone or in community with others, and both in public and in private, to manifest and propagate his or her religion or belief in worship, teaching, practice and observance.

### Section 11 - Expression

No person shall be hindered by government in the enjoyment of his or her freedom of expression, which includes freedom to hold opinions and to receive and impart ideas and information without interference, and freedom from interference with his or her correspondence or other means of communication.

Nothing in this section shall be held to prevent the Government from making reasonable provision that is required in the interests of defence, public safety, public order, public morality or public health.

### Section 12 - Assembly and Association

No person shall be hindered by government in the enjoyment of his or her freedom of peaceful assembly and association, that is to say, his or her right peaceably to assemble freely and to associate with other persons and in particular to form or belong to political parties, trade unions or other associations for the protection of his or her interests.

### Section 13 - Movement

No person shall be deprived by government of his or her freedom of movement, that is to say, the right to move freely throughout the Cayman Islands, the right to reside in any part of the Cayman Islands, the right to enter the Cayman Islands, the right to leave the Cayman Islands, and immunity from expulsion from the Cayman Islands.

### Section 14 - Marriage

Government shall respect the right of every unmarried man and woman of marriageable age (as determined by law) freely to marry a person of the opposite sex and found a family.

### Section 15 - Property

Government shall not deprive any person of his or her property except where any taking of property is in accordance with the law and necessary for a purpose that is in the public interest.

Where any property is so taken, government shall promptly pay the person full and fair compensation for the taking.

### Section 16 - Non-discrimination

Subject to the provisions of this section, government shall not treat any person in a discriminatory manner in respect of the rights under this Part of the Constitution.

In this section, discriminatory means affording different treatment to different persons attributable wholly or mainly to their respective descriptions by sex, race, colour, language, religion, political or other opinion, national or social origin, association with a national minority, property, birth or other status.

### Section 17 - Protection of Children

Every child has the right to such measures of protection as are required by his or her status as a minor on the part of his or her family, government and society.

Government shall take measures to ensure that every child has the right to food, clothing and shelter, to health care, to education, and to be protected from abuse, neglect, violence and exploitation.

### Section 18 - Protection of the Environment

Government shall, in all its decisions, have due regard to the need to foster the health, safety and environmental well-being of the people of the Cayman Islands.

Government shall foster an environment that is not harmful to the health or well-being of present and future generations and shall establish appropriate agencies to monitor and regulate the activities of persons who affect the environment.

### Section 19 - Lawful Administrative Action

All decisions and acts of public officials must be lawful, rational, proportionate and procedurally fair.

Every person whose interests have been adversely affected by such a decision or act has the right to be furnished with reasons for that decision or act.

### Section 20 - Education

Government shall seek to achieve in the Cayman Islands the provision of primary and secondary education which shall, so far as government is able, be free.

No person shall be denied the right to education.

Government shall respect the right of parents to ensure education and teaching of their children in conformity with their religious and philosophical convictions.

### Section 21 - Public Emergencies

The Governor may, by proclamation which shall be published in the Gazette, declare that a state of public emergency exists for the purposes of this Part whenever he or she is satisfied that a public emergency has arisen as a result of the occurrence of any earthquake, hurricane, flood, fire, outbreak of pestilence, outbreak of infectious disease or other calamity whether similar to the foregoing or not, or that action has been taken or is immediately threatened by any person or body of persons of such a nature and on so extensive a scale as to be likely to endanger the public safety or to deprive the community, or any substantial portion of the community, of supplies or services essential to life.

### Section 22 - Protection of Persons Detained Under Emergency Laws

When a person is detained by virtue of any such law as is referred to in section 21, the following provisions shall apply: the detainee shall be informed of the grounds of detention and be permitted to make representations; a tribunal shall review the detention within a reasonable time; the detainee shall be afforded reasonable facilities for consulting a legal representative.

### Section 23 - Declaration of Incompatibility

If a court is satisfied that a provision of any law is incompatible with any of the rights or freedoms set out in this Part, it may make a declaration of that incompatibility.

Where a declaration of incompatibility is made, the law in question continues in force and effect, and is not invalidated, and the Legislature shall reconsider the provision.

### Section 24 - Duty of Public Officials

It is unlawful for a public official to make a decision or to act in a way that is incompatible with the Bill of Rights, Freedoms and Responsibilities.

### Section 25 - Interpretive Obligation

So far as it is possible to do so, all legislation, whenever enacted, must be read and given effect in a way which is compatible with the rights set out in this Part.

### Section 26 - Enforcement of Rights and Freedoms

If any person alleges that any of the provisions of this Part has been, is being or is likely to be contravened in relation to him or her, then, without prejudice to any other action with respect to the same matter which is lawfully available, that person may apply to the Grand Court for redress.

The Grand Court shall have original jurisdiction to hear and determine any application made by any person in pursuance of this section and may make such declarations and orders, issue such writs and give such directions as it may consider appropriate for the purpose of enforcing or securing the enforcement of any of the provisions of this Part.

### Section 27 - Remedies

A court may grant such relief or remedy, or may make such order, in respect of any contravention or threatened contravention of this Part as it considers just and appropriate.

Damages shall be awarded only where a court is satisfied that the award is necessary to afford just and effective relief.

### Section 28 - Interpretation of the Bill of Rights

In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires: "Caymanian" means a person who possesses Caymanian status under the law in force in the Cayman Islands or who is entitled to be registered as such; "court" means any court of law in the Cayman Islands including the Grand Court, the Court of Appeal, and any subordinate court; "disciplined force" means a naval, military or air force, the police force or the prison service; "public official" means a person who exercises a public function, including a member of the Legislative Assembly, a member of the Cabinet, a judge or magistrate, or any other person exercising functions on behalf of government.

---

## Part II - The Governor

### Section 29 - The Office of Governor

There shall be a Governor of the Cayman Islands who shall be appointed by Her Majesty by Commission under Her Sign Manual and Signet and shall hold office during Her Majesty's pleasure.

### Section 30 - Oaths

A person appointed to hold the office of Governor shall, before entering upon the duties of that office, take and subscribe the oath of allegiance and such oath for the due execution of the office of Governor as may be prescribed.

The said oaths shall be administered by the Chief Justice or, in his or her absence, by such other person as the Governor may appoint.

### Section 31 - Functions

The Governor shall exercise his or her functions in accordance with the provisions of this Constitution and of any other law and, to the extent that such functions are not so prescribed, in such manner as the Governor shall think fit.

The Governor shall in particular take such steps as he or she considers appropriate for promoting good governance and the well-being of the people of the Cayman Islands.

### Section 32 - Exercise of Functions

In the exercise of any function in respect of which it is provided by this Constitution that the Governor shall act in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet, the Governor shall, so far as is practicable, consult with the Cabinet before making any decision.

The Governor shall not be obliged to act in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet in the exercise of any function which, by this Constitution or any other law, is expressed to be exercisable by the Governor in his or her discretion or in accordance with the instructions of a Secretary of State.

### Section 33 - Acting Contrary to Cabinet Advice

In any case in which the Governor is by this Constitution directed to act in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet, the Governor may act otherwise than in accordance with that advice if he or she is instructed to do so by a Secretary of State, or if, in his or her judgment, compliance with such advice would involve any infringement of any of the special responsibilities of the Governor.

### Section 34 - Deputy Governor

There shall be a Deputy Governor of the Cayman Islands who shall be appointed by Her Majesty.

A person shall not be appointed as Deputy Governor unless he or she is a Caymanian and has considerable experience of public service within the Cayman Islands.

The Deputy Governor shall be the head of the civil service of the Cayman Islands.

### Section 35 - Acting Governor

Whenever the office of Governor is vacant or the holder of the office is absent from the Cayman Islands or is for any reason unable to perform the functions of the office, the Deputy Governor shall perform those functions.

If the Deputy Governor is also absent or unable to act, the Governor may designate a public officer to perform the functions of the office.

### Section 36 - Temporary Function Exercise

Whenever the Governor is absent from the Cayman Islands or considers it desirable to do so for any reason, he or she may, by instrument under the public seal, appoint the Deputy Governor or any other Caymanian public officer to perform on his or her behalf such of the functions of the office of Governor as may be specified.

### Section 37 - Emoluments and Staff

The Governor shall receive such emoluments and shall be provided with such staff as may be fixed by a Secretary of State.

The Legislature may by law prescribe the staffing and expenditure arrangements for the office of the Governor.

### Section 38 - Powers to Dispose of Land

The Governor may, in Her Majesty's name and on Her Majesty's behalf, make and execute under the public seal grants and dispositions of any lands within the Cayman Islands that may lawfully be granted or disposed of by Her Majesty.

### Section 39 - Powers of Pardon

The Governor may, in Her Majesty's name and on Her Majesty's behalf: grant to any person convicted of any offence a pardon, either free or subject to lawful conditions; grant to any person a respite of the execution of any punishment imposed on that person for such offence; substitute a less severe form of punishment for that imposed by any sentence; or remit the whole or any part of any sentence of imprisonment or any fine, penalty or forfeiture due to the Crown.

### Section 40 - Advisory Committee on Prerogative of Mercy

There shall be an Advisory Committee on the Prerogative of Mercy which shall consist of the Attorney General, the Chief Medical Officer, and not more than four other members appointed by the Governor.

The Committee shall advise the Governor on the exercise of the power of pardon.

### Section 41 - Public Seal

The Governor shall keep and use the public seal for sealing all things that shall pass the said seal.

### Section 42 - Constitution of Offices

The Governor may constitute and appoint persons to hold or act in the offices within the Government of the Cayman Islands that may lawfully be constituted by the Governor in Her Majesty's name.

---

## Part III - The Executive

### Section 43 - Executive Authority

The executive authority of the Cayman Islands is vested in Her Majesty.

Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the executive authority of the Cayman Islands shall be exercised on behalf of Her Majesty by the Government of the Cayman Islands, which shall consist of the Governor acting on the advice of the Cabinet.

### Section 44 - The Cabinet

There shall be a Cabinet for the Cayman Islands which shall consist of the Premier, not less than six other Ministers appointed in accordance with section 49 (one of whom shall be designated as the Deputy Premier), and, as ex officio members, the Deputy Governor and the Attorney General.

The Cabinet shall be the principal instrument of policy and shall be charged with the general direction and control of the government of the Cayman Islands and shall be collectively responsible to the Legislative Assembly for any advice given to the Governor.

### Section 45 - Meetings

The Cabinet shall meet at such times and at such places as may be necessary or expedient for the transaction of business, and such meetings shall be held at the direction of the Premier or whenever requested by the Governor.

The Cabinet Secretary shall summon meetings of the Cabinet.

### Section 46 - Proceedings and Quorum

The Governor shall, so far as is practicable, preside at meetings of the Cabinet.

In the absence of the Governor, the Premier shall preside.

The Governor and the Premier shall agree on the agenda for meetings of the Cabinet.

A quorum of the Cabinet shall be a majority of the Ministers.

### Section 47 - Attendance

The person presiding at a meeting of the Cabinet may summon any public officer to attend such meeting where in his or her opinion the business before the Cabinet renders the officer's presence desirable.

An elected member of the Legislative Assembly for an electoral district may attend a meeting of the Cabinet once every quarter to make representations about matters relating to his or her district.

### Section 48 - Cabinet Secretary

There shall be a Cabinet Office, the head of which shall be the Cabinet Secretary.

The Cabinet Secretary shall be a Caymanian and shall be appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier.

The Cabinet Secretary shall have charge of the Cabinet Office and shall be responsible, in accordance with such instructions as may be given to him or her by the Governor or the Premier, for arranging the business of and keeping the minutes of the Cabinet, and for conveying the conclusions of the Cabinet to the appropriate person or authority.

### Section 49 - Appointment of Premier and Ministers

The Governor shall appoint as Premier the elected member of the Legislative Assembly who is best able to command the confidence of a majority of the elected members of the Assembly.

The Governor shall, acting in accordance with the advice of the Premier, appoint the other Ministers from among the elected members of the Legislative Assembly.

### Section 50 - Functions of Premier

The Premier shall exercise such functions as are conferred on him or her by this Constitution and any other law.

The Premier shall exercise his or her functions in the best interests of the Cayman Islands.

### Section 51 - Tenure of Premier

If the Legislative Assembly passes a resolution supported by the votes of not less than two-thirds of the elected members declaring that it has no confidence in the Premier, the Governor shall remove the Premier from office.

The Premier shall also vacate his or her office when after any general election the Governor appoints a person to the office of Premier.

### Section 52 - Tenure of Ministers

A Minister other than the Premier shall vacate his or her office if he or she ceases to be a member of the Legislative Assembly, if his or her appointment is revoked by the Governor acting in accordance with the advice of the Premier, if the Premier resigns from office or is removed from office, or if the Minister is absent from the Cayman Islands for a period exceeding such period as may be prescribed by Standing Orders without the leave of the Governor.

### Section 53 - Performance of Functions in Certain Events

Whenever the Premier is absent from the Cayman Islands or is by reason of illness unable to perform the functions of his or her office, the Governor shall authorise the Deputy Premier or some other Minister to perform those functions.

### Section 54 - Allocation of Responsibilities

The Governor, acting in accordance with the advice of the Premier, shall by directions in writing charge any Minister with responsibility for the conduct of any business of the Government of the Cayman Islands.

One of the Ministers shall be charged with responsibility for finance.

All Ministers shall be collectively responsible for all measures approved by the Cabinet.

### Section 55 - Special Responsibilities of Governor

The Governor shall be responsible for the conduct of any business of the Government of the Cayman Islands with respect to defence, external affairs (subject to the provisions of this Constitution), internal security (including the police), and the appointment, removal, discipline and terms and conditions of service of public officers.

### Section 56 - Attorney General

There shall be an Attorney General of the Cayman Islands, whose office shall be a public office and who shall be appointed in accordance with section 106.

The Attorney General shall be the principal legal adviser to the Government of the Cayman Islands and to the Legislative Assembly.

### Section 57 - Director of Public Prosecutions

There shall be a Director of Public Prosecutions whose office shall be a public office.

The Director of Public Prosecutions shall have power to institute and undertake criminal proceedings against any person before any court in respect of any offence against any law in force in the Cayman Islands.

The Director of Public Prosecutions may take over and continue any criminal proceedings that have been instituted by any other person or authority, and may discontinue at any stage before judgment is delivered any criminal proceedings instituted or undertaken by himself or herself or any other person or authority.

In the exercise of his or her powers under this section, the Director of Public Prosecutions shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority.

### Section 58 - National Security Council

There shall be a National Security Council which shall consist of the Governor as Chairman, the Premier, two other Ministers nominated by the Premier, a person nominated by the Leader of the Opposition, and two persons appointed by the Governor from civil society, together with, as ex officio members, the Deputy Governor, the Attorney General and the Commissioner of Police.

The National Security Council shall be responsible for advising the Governor on matters relating to internal security, but not on operational matters.

---

## Part IV - The Legislature

### Section 59 - Composition of the Legislature and Power to Make Laws

There shall be a Legislature of the Cayman Islands which shall consist of Her Majesty and a Legislative Assembly.

The Legislature shall have power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Cayman Islands.

### Section 60 - The Legislative Assembly

The Legislative Assembly shall comprise a Speaker, eighteen elected members representing such electoral districts as may be prescribed by law, and, as ex officio members, the Deputy Governor and the Attorney General.

The number of elected members may be increased from time to time in accordance with the recommendations of an Electoral Boundary Commission.

### Section 61 - Qualifications for Elected Membership

A person shall be qualified to be elected as a member of the Legislative Assembly if, and shall not be so qualified unless, he or she is a Caymanian, has attained the age of twenty-one years, is domiciled and resident in the Cayman Islands, and is otherwise qualified as a citizen.

### Section 62 - Disqualifications for Elected Membership

A person shall not be qualified to be elected as a member of the Legislative Assembly if that person acknowledges allegiance to any foreign power or state, holds or is acting in any public office, is an undischarged bankrupt, is certified to be insane or otherwise adjudged to be of unsound mind, is under sentence of death or is serving a sentence of imprisonment exceeding twelve months, or holds any office connected with the conduct of elections.

### Section 63 - Tenure of Office of Elected Members

The seat of an elected member of the Legislative Assembly shall become vacant upon the dissolution of the Assembly, if the member is absent from sittings of the Assembly for such period and in such circumstances as may be prescribed by Standing Orders, if the member ceases to be a Caymanian, or if the member resigns his or her seat.

### Section 64 - Delay in Vacation of Seat to Allow for Appeal

Where an elected member of the Legislative Assembly is subject to any disqualification, the member shall immediately cease to perform his or her functions as a member but shall not be required to vacate his or her seat until the expiration of thirty days from the date of the disqualifying event.

The Governor may extend the period for an appeal.

### Section 65 - Speaker and Deputy Speaker

The elected members of the Legislative Assembly shall elect a Speaker and a Deputy Speaker from among the elected members or from among persons who are qualified for election as members but are not members of the Assembly.

The Speaker and Deputy Speaker shall not be Ministers.

### Section 66 - Determination of Questions as to Membership

The Grand Court shall have exclusive jurisdiction to determine any question whether any person has been validly elected as a member of the Legislative Assembly or whether the seat of any elected member has become vacant.

Any such determination shall be final and not subject to appeal.

### Section 67 - Penalty for Sitting or Voting While Unqualified

Any person who sits or votes in the Legislative Assembly knowing or having reasonable grounds for knowing that he or she is not entitled to do so shall be liable to a penalty not exceeding five hundred dollars for each day on which he or she so sits or votes.

### Section 68 - Leader of the Opposition

The Governor shall appoint as Leader of the Opposition the person who, in the judgment of the Governor, is best able to command the support of the largest number of opposition members.

The Leader of the Opposition shall vacate his or her office when the member ceases to be a member of the Assembly, after a general election, if the member becomes a member of the Cabinet, or if the member resigns.

### Section 69 - Power to Provide for a Referendum

The Legislature may, by resolution of the Legislative Assembly supported by a majority of the elected members, authorise the holding of a referendum on any matter of national importance.

Any matter relating to the status of the Cayman Islands as regards independence shall be deemed to be a matter of national importance.

### Section 70 - People-Initiated Referendums

The Legislature shall enact provisions for the holding of a referendum on any matter of national importance that does not contravene the Bill of Rights, Freedoms and Responsibilities or any other provision of this Constitution, when initiated by a petition signed by not less than twenty-five per cent of persons registered as electors.

The result of a referendum held under this section shall be binding on the Government and the Legislative Assembly if more than fifty per cent of all persons registered as electors have voted in the referendum and have approved the proposition.

### Section 71 - Standing Orders and Committees

Subject to the provisions of this Constitution, the Legislative Assembly may, with the approval of the Governor, make Standing Orders for the regulation and orderly conduct of its proceedings.

There shall be established a Finance Committee which shall examine the proposals of the Government for the expenditure of public monies.

### Section 72 - Presiding in the Legislative Assembly

The Speaker shall preside at any sitting of the Legislative Assembly.

In the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker shall preside.

In the absence of both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker, such elected member of the Assembly (not being a Minister) as the Assembly may elect for that purpose shall preside.

### Section 73 - Legislative Assembly May Transact Business Notwithstanding Vacancies

The Legislative Assembly shall not be disqualified for the transaction of business by reason of any vacancy in the membership thereof, and any proceedings in the Assembly shall be valid notwithstanding that some person who was not entitled to do so sat or voted in the Assembly.

### Section 74 - Quorum

If at any time during a sitting of the Legislative Assembly the Speaker is satisfied that fewer than a majority of the elected members of the Assembly, together with the person presiding, are present, the Speaker shall adjourn the Assembly.

### Section 75 - Voting

Save as otherwise provided in this Constitution, all questions proposed for decision in the Legislative Assembly shall be determined by a majority of the votes of the members present and voting.

The person presiding shall have no original vote but shall have and exercise a casting vote in the case of an equality of votes.

The Deputy Governor and the Attorney General shall not vote.

### Section 76 - Summoning of Persons to Assist the Legislative Assembly

The person presiding in the Legislative Assembly may summon any person to attend a meeting of the Assembly when in his or her opinion the business before the Assembly renders the presence of that person desirable.

### Section 77 - Introduction of Bills

Any member of the Legislative Assembly may introduce a bill, motion or petition in the Assembly, and the same shall be debated and disposed of according to Standing Orders.

A bill for an Act imposing or increasing any tax, charge or duty shall not be introduced except on the recommendation of the Minister charged with responsibility for finance.

### Section 78 - Assent to Bills

A bill shall not become law until the Governor has assented to it in Her Majesty's name and has signed it in token of such assent.

The Governor may reserve a bill for the signification of Her Majesty's pleasure if it appears to him or her that the bill is in any way repugnant to or inconsistent with any provision of this Constitution.

### Section 79 - Return of Bills by the Governor

Where a bill has been presented to the Governor for his or her assent, the Governor may, within sixty days, return the bill to the Legislative Assembly with a message requesting that the Assembly reconsider the bill or any specified provision thereof and, in particular, any such amendments as he or she may recommend.

### Section 80 - Disallowance of Laws

Any law to which the Governor has given his or her assent may be disallowed by Her Majesty through a Secretary of State. When such disallowance is notified to the Governor and published in the Gazette, the law shall thereupon cease to have effect.

### Section 81 - Governor's Reserved Power

If at any time the Governor considers that it is necessary in order to discharge any of the Governor's responsibilities for a law to be enacted that the Cabinet will not support, the Governor may, with the prior approval of a Secretary of State, cause to be published the text of such a bill as a Government Notice and may declare that the bill shall have effect as if it had been passed by the Legislative Assembly and assented to by the Governor.

### Section 82 - Privileges of the Legislative Assembly and Its Members

The Legislature may determine the privileges, immunities and powers of the Legislative Assembly and the members thereof; but no such privileges, immunities or powers shall exceed those for the time being held or enjoyed by the Commons House of Parliament of the United Kingdom or by the members thereof.

### Section 83 - Sessions of the Legislative Assembly

There shall be a session of the Legislative Assembly at such place within the Cayman Islands and beginning at such time as the Governor may by proclamation appoint, and such sessions shall be held at least once in every year.

### Section 84 - Prorogation and Dissolution

The Governor may at any time prorogue or dissolve the Legislative Assembly.

The Governor shall dissolve the Legislative Assembly at the expiration of four years from the date of the first sitting of the Assembly after any general election.

### Section 85 - Recalling Dissolved Legislative Assembly

Where between a dissolution of the Legislative Assembly and the next following general election a matter arises which in the opinion of the Governor requires the recall of the dissolved Assembly, the Governor may summon the Assembly that has been dissolved, and that Assembly shall thereupon be deemed to be the Legislative Assembly until the date of the next general election.

### Section 86 - General Elections and Bye-Elections

A general election of members of the Legislative Assembly shall be held at such time within two months after every dissolution of the Assembly as the Governor shall appoint by proclamation.

Whenever a vacancy occurs in the seat of an elected member of the Legislative Assembly, a bye-election shall be held within two months.

### Section 87 - Public Accounts Committee

As soon as practicable after each general election, the elected members of the Legislative Assembly shall elect from among their number a Public Accounts Committee.

The Public Accounts Committee shall examine the accounts of the Government and shall have access to all books, records, returns, reports and other documents relating or relevant to those accounts.

### Section 88 - Electoral Boundary Commission

The Governor shall from time to time appoint an Electoral Boundary Commission which shall consist of a Chairman appointed by the Governor and not more than four other members.

The Commission shall review the boundaries of the electoral districts and may make recommendations for changes.

### Section 89 - Review and Alteration of Electoral District Boundaries

An Electoral Boundary Commission shall review the boundaries of the electoral districts and may make recommendations for changes to those boundaries.

The Commission shall ensure that the ratio of elected members to persons qualified to be registered as electors in each electoral district is, so far as is reasonably practicable, the same in each district.

### Section 90 - Qualifications of Electors

Every person shall be entitled to be registered as an elector in one electoral district if, and shall not be entitled to be so registered unless, he or she is a Caymanian, has attained the age of eighteen years, is resident in the Cayman Islands, and has been resident in the Cayman Islands for a period or periods amounting to not less than two years out of the four years immediately preceding the date of registration.

### Section 91 - Disqualifications of Electors

No person shall be entitled to be registered as an elector who is serving a sentence of imprisonment of twelve months or more, is certified to be insane or otherwise adjudged to be of unsound mind, or is disqualified for registration as an elector by any law relating to offences connected with elections.

### Section 92 - Right to Vote at Elections

Every person registered as an elector in an electoral district shall be entitled to vote at any election of a member or members of the Legislative Assembly for that electoral district.

No person shall vote at any election in more than one electoral district.

### Section 93 - Law as to Elections

The Legislature may make provision for the registration of electors, the qualification of candidates for election, the division of the Cayman Islands into electoral districts, and the conduct of elections.

---

## Part V - The Judicature

### Section 94 - Constitution and Jurisdiction of the Grand Court

There shall be a Grand Court for the Cayman Islands which shall be a superior Court of Record and shall have such jurisdiction and powers as may be conferred on it by this Constitution and any other law.

The Court shall have an official seal bearing its name and a device approved by the Chief Justice.

### Section 95 - Composition of the Grand Court

The Grand Court shall consist of a Chief Justice and such number of other judges as may be prescribed by law.

All the judges of the Grand Court, including the Chief Justice, shall be appointed by the Governor by instrument under the public seal in accordance with section 106.

The emoluments of the judges shall be prescribed by law and charged upon the revenues of the Cayman Islands, and shall not be reduced without the consent of the judge concerned.

### Section 96 - Tenure of Office of Judges of the Grand Court

A judge of the Grand Court shall vacate his or her office when he or she attains the age of sixty-five years, but the Governor, acting on the recommendation of the Judicial and Legal Services Commission, may permit a judge who has attained that age to continue in office for such period as may be necessary to enable him or her to deliver judgment or to do any other thing in relation to proceedings that were commenced before him or her before he or she attained that age.

A judge may be removed from office only for inability to discharge the functions of his or her office or for misbehaviour.

### Section 97 - Acting Judges of the Grand Court

If the office of Chief Justice is vacant or if the Chief Justice is for any reason unable to perform the functions of his or her office, then such other person as may be qualified for appointment as a judge may be appointed by the Governor to act as Chief Justice.

### Section 98 - Oaths to be Taken by Judges of the Grand Court

A judge of the Grand Court shall, before entering upon the duties of his or her office, take and subscribe the oath of allegiance and the judicial oath.

### Section 99 - Constitution and Jurisdiction of the Court of Appeal

There shall be a Court of Appeal for the Cayman Islands which shall be a superior Court of Record and shall have jurisdiction and powers to hear and determine such appeals from the Grand Court as may be prescribed by this Constitution or any other law.

### Section 100 - Composition of the Court of Appeal

The Court of Appeal shall consist of a President and not less than two Justices of Appeal, appointed by the Governor in accordance with section 106.

A judge of the Court of Appeal shall be a person who holds or has held high judicial office.

### Section 101 - Tenure of Office of Judges of the Court of Appeal

A judge of the Court of Appeal shall hold office for such period as may be specified in his or her instrument of appointment.

A judge may be removed from office only for inability to discharge the functions of his or her office or for misbehaviour.

### Section 102 - Acting Judges of the Court of Appeal

If the office of President of the Court of Appeal is vacant or if the President is unable to perform the functions of his or her office, the Governor may appoint a person qualified for appointment as a Justice of Appeal to act as President.

### Section 103 - Oaths to be Taken by Judges of the Court of Appeal

A judge of the Court of Appeal shall, before entering upon the duties of his or her office, take and subscribe the oath of allegiance and the judicial oath.

### Section 104 - Subordinate Courts

A law enacted by the Legislature may establish courts subordinate to the Grand Court.

The Grand Court shall have supervisory jurisdiction over the proceedings of subordinate courts and may make such orders, issue such writs and give such directions as it may consider appropriate for the purpose of ensuring that justice is duly administered by such courts.

### Section 105 - Judicial and Legal Services Commission

There shall be a Judicial and Legal Services Commission which shall consist of a Chairman who is not a legal practitioner and one other member, both appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier and the Leader of the Opposition; the President of the Court of Appeal; and other members as prescribed by this Constitution.

The Commission shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority.

### Section 106 - Functions of the Judicial and Legal Services Commission

The Governor shall make appointments to judicial and legal offices in accordance with the advice of the Judicial and Legal Services Commission.

The offices to which this section applies include the Chief Justice, judges of the Grand Court, the President and judges of the Court of Appeal, the Attorney General, the Director of Public Prosecutions, and Magistrates.

### Section 107 - Judicial Administration

The Legislature and the Cabinet shall uphold the rule of law and judicial independence, and shall ensure that adequate funds are provided to support the judicial administration in the Cayman Islands.

---

## Part VI - The Public Service

### Section 108 - Overriding Duty of Public Officers

Subject to this Constitution, all public officers must act in accordance with the best interests of the Cayman Islands and not in their own private interests, and implement Government policy to the best of their ability and in accordance with the directions given to them by the Cabinet or other responsible person or authority.

### Section 109 - Appointment of Public Officers

Power to make appointments to public offices, and to transfer, remove or exercise disciplinary control over persons holding or acting in such offices, is, subject to this Constitution, vested in the Governor acting in his or her discretion.

The Governor may, by regulations, delegate such powers subject to specified conditions.

### Section 110 - Applicability of Pensions Law

The law applicable to the grant and payment to any public officer, or to his or her dependants, of any pension, compensation, gratuity or other like allowance in respect of the service of that officer shall be the law that was in force on the relevant date or any law not less favourable to the person concerned.

---

## Part VII - Finance

### Section 111 - Revenue and Expenditure

The Legislature shall have the authority to levy or change the rates of revenue unless otherwise provided by law.

No expenditure of public money, no acquisition of assets and no incurrence of liabilities by government shall be authorised except by the Legislature by appropriation or by any other law.

A law enacted by the Legislature shall govern the operation of the Government's financial system and processes.

### Section 112 - Reporting

At least one report annually shall be made to the Legislative Assembly on the Government's financial performance and fiscal position.

A law enacted by the Legislature shall provide for the content, timing and process for financial reporting to the Legislative Assembly.

### Section 113 - Public Debt

Total Government borrowing shall not exceed an amount where the annual cost of servicing the debt and repaying the principal thereof amounts to more than such percentage of Government revenue as may be set by law and agreed with a Secretary of State.

A law enacted by the Legislature may provide for a higher percentage for a limited period where a matter arises of such urgency that in the opinion of the Cabinet it is necessary.

### Section 114 - Auditor General

There shall be an Auditor General for the Cayman Islands who shall be appointed by the Governor.

The Auditor General shall audit and report on the public accounts of the Cayman Islands and shall undertake such other value-for-money investigations as he or she considers appropriate.

In the exercise of his or her functions, the Auditor General shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority, save that the Auditor General is answerable to the Public Accounts Committee.

### Section 115 - Financial Secretary

There shall be a Financial Secretary of the Cayman Islands who shall serve as the principal financial adviser to the Minister charged with responsibility for finance and shall hold a public office.

---

## Part VIII - Institutions Supporting Democracy

### Section 116 - Human Rights Commission

There shall be in and for the Cayman Islands a Human Rights Commission whose primary responsibility shall be the promotion of understanding and observance of human rights in the Cayman Islands.

The Commission shall consist of a Chairman and four other members appointed by the Governor after consulting the Premier and the Leader of the Opposition.

The Commission shall have power to receive and investigate complaints of breaches of rights, provide advice, mediate disputes, and issue reports on human rights matters.

The Commission shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority.

### Section 117 - Commission for Standards in Public Life

There shall be a Commission for Standards in Public Life which shall consist of a Chairman and between two and four other members appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier and the Leader of the Opposition.

The functions of the Commission shall include setting standards of integrity and ethical conduct for public officials, monitoring and investigating compliance, supervising registers of interests, and reporting to the Legislative Assembly.

### Section 118 - Constitutional Commission

There shall be a Constitutional Commission comprising a Chairman and two other members, at least one of whom shall be an experienced lawyer.

The Commission shall advise the Government on constitutional matters, publish reports and discussion papers, and promote constitutional awareness.

The Commission shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority.

### Section 119 - Advisory District Councils

The Legislature shall establish advisory councils for electoral districts to assist the elected members of the Legislative Assembly for those districts.

### Section 120 - Complaints Commissioner

The Legislature may establish the office of Complaints Commissioner with powers and functions to investigate complaints of maladministration.

The Complaints Commissioner shall not be subject to the direction or control of any other person or authority.

### Section 121 - Register of Interests

The Commission for Standards in Public Life shall maintain a Register of Interests.

Every member of the Legislative Assembly and every person holding prescribed public office shall, upon taking office and at regular intervals, declare to the Commission his or her interests, assets, income and liabilities.

### Section 122 - Freedom of Information

The Legislature shall make provision for the right of access to official documents held by public authorities, subject to such conditions and exceptions as are necessary in a democratic society.

---

## Part IX - Miscellaneous

### Section 123 - Official Language of the Cayman Islands

The official language of the Cayman Islands is English.

### Section 124 - Interpretation

In this Constitution, unless the context otherwise requires: "Assembly" means the Legislative Assembly; "Bill of Rights" means Part I of this Constitution; "Caymanian" means a person who possesses Caymanian status under the law in force in the Cayman Islands; "Court of Appeal" means the Court of Appeal established by section 99; "functions" includes powers, duties and responsibilities; "Government" means the Government of the Cayman Islands; "Governor" means the Governor of the Cayman Islands; "Grand Court" means the Grand Court established by section 94; "high judicial office" means the office of a judge of a court having unlimited jurisdiction in civil and criminal matters; "law" includes any instrument having the force of law; "Legislature" means the Legislature of the Cayman Islands established by section 59; "Minister" means a member of the Cabinet other than an ex officio member; "Premier" means the Premier appointed under section 49; "public office" means any office of emolument in the public service; "public officer" means a person holding or acting in any public office.

### Section 125 - Power Reserved to Her Majesty

There is reserved to Her Majesty full power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Cayman Islands.

---

End of the Constitution of the Cayman Islands.
